scenario: A mid-market HR software company is considering whether to raise prices by 18% to improve margins and fund product innovation, but the Sales team warns that 30% of the customer base will churn if prices rise. Product leadership argues the feature set has improved 40% since the last price increase 18 months ago, justifying the raise. Customer Success reports high satisfaction scores but notes many customers have built dependency on the product and have limited alternatives. The CEO must decide between maximizing short-term revenue or protecting customer relationships.

manas_signals.description: Product team wants investment capital to build differentiated features; current margins are insufficient to fund roadmap.
Sales team fears losing large strategic accounts and market share if competitors exploit price differential to poach customers.
Industry benchmarks show similar companies have raised prices 12-20% over 24 months without major churn; the 40% feature improvement supports a meaningful increase.
Segmented pricing (pro/enterprise tiers) could allow price increase for new customers while grandfathering existing ones, spreading impact over time.

buddhi_options.option: Implement a 12% price increase for new customers and a 6% increase for renewals (starting 90 days out); grandfather existing customers at current price for 12 more months to soften churn impact.
buddhi_options.reasoning: Achieves ~90% of revenue target while limiting customer disruption; allows time for value realization; grandfathering demonstrates customer loyalty; renewal approach is standard practice.

buddhi_options.option: Implement the full 18% increase immediately across all segments; use cost savings to fund aggressive customer success initiatives to reduce churn to 10-12%.
buddhi_options.reasoning: Maximizes short-term revenue but ignores Sales' churn warning; high-touch support cannot overcome perceived value-to-price mismatch for price-sensitive segments.

decision_rationale: A phased, segmented pricing approach honors both the business need for margin improvement and the relational obligation to long-standing customers, allowing the company to demonstrate that it values loyalty while still capturing fair value for improved product. This elevates organizational maturity (target 1) by showing restraint and strategic thinking rather than maximum short-term extraction.

scenario: PublishingCorp, a 75-year-old academic publishing house, is evaluating a proposal to divest its print journal business (currently 35% of revenue, 12% operating margins) to focus entirely on digital publishing and open-access models. The print business is stable but declining 8% annually; digital is growing 22% annually but currently has 6% margins. The sale would generate $45M in upfront capital. Print business has deep customer relationships and staff with decades of institutional knowledge. The board is split between growth-focused members advocating divestment and traditionalists concerned about abandoning core competency and long-term relationships.

manas_signals.description: Digital-first strategy aligns with market trends and could unlock higher margin opportunities through technology and data leverage.
Publishing industry has seen major transitions with print declining and digital-native publishers emerging as market leaders.
Long-serving employees and academic community relationships may be disrupted; concern about loss of institutional knowledge and editorial quality.
Digital margins are currently lower than print; growth projections may not materialize, leaving company with lower-margin business and one-time capital infusion.

buddhi_options.option: Divest print journal business to investor consortium, creating spin-off company that maintains long-term relationships while allowing PublishingCorp to focus on high-growth digital models.
buddhi_options.reasoning: Honors legacy business and relationships through continuity; enables organizational focus on growth areas; demonstrates respect for heritage while pursuing strategic evolution.

buddhi_options.option: Retain print business but gradually reduce investment while aggressively shifting resources to digital, allowing market forces to naturally retire print over 5-7 years.
buddhi_options.reasoning: Maintains stakeholder relationships and optionality; reduces disruption and allows smoother transition; may result in suboptimal capital allocation if digital growth accelerates.

buddhi_options.option: Execute rapid divestment of print business, using $45M capital to aggressively acquire digital publishing assets and hire technology talent to build comprehensive digital platform.
buddhi_options.reasoning: Maximizes growth potential but creates organizational disruption and abandons long-term stakeholder relationships; assumes acquisition strategy and digital growth projections will materialize.

buddhi_options.option: Maintain integrated model but separate print and digital into distinct business units with independent P&Ls, P&L accountability, and market-based transfer pricing.
buddhi_options.reasoning: Creates organizational clarity and performance accountability; acknowledges both businesses have merit; may result in inefficiencies if shared services are not well-designed.

decision_rationale: The decision to divest while structuring the spin-off to maintain print business continuity honored the organization's 75-year heritage and academic relationships while enabling strategic focus on growth markets. The spin-off preserved 85% of print business jobs and maintained editorial quality standards, creating a separate viable enterprise. PublishingCorp successfully transitioned 60% of revenue to digital within 4 years, achieving 18% digital margins while the print spin-off remained profitable, satisfying both growth and stakeholder responsibility objectives.

scenario: A mid-market SaaS company has developed a strong customer base in healthcare compliance software, but a well-funded competitor has just launched a similar product at 40% lower pricing. The sales team reports that three major deals are at risk. The executive team must decide within two weeks whether to match pricing, invest in differentiation, or focus on customer retention through service excellence. The CFO warns that aggressive pricing will compress margins significantly, while the Head of Product advocates for accelerating a planned AI-powered feature roadmap.

manas_signals.description: Risk of rapid customer defection to lower-cost competitor threatens revenue stability and market position.
Competitor's aggressive entry may signal market validation and demand surge that justifies product investment.
Historical data shows healthcare buyers prioritize compliance assurance and integration over price in vendor selection.
Sales team urgency and conflicting internal opinions create pressure to react without strategic clarity.

buddhi_options.option: Match competitor pricing on entry-level tiers while investing heavily in differentiated AI features and exclusive compliance certifications.
buddhi_options.reasoning: Balances short-term customer retention with long-term competitive advantage; demonstrates commitment to customer value while building sustainable moat aligned with company mission.

buddhi_options.option: Maintain current pricing and focus exclusively on customer success, deepening relationships through premium support and custom integrations.
buddhi_options.reasoning: Respects existing customer relationships and margins; builds loyalty through value delivery rather than price competition, supporting sustainable growth.

buddhi_options.option: Aggressively cut pricing by 35% to undercut competitor and capture market share before they establish foothold.
buddhi_options.reasoning: Short-term customer acquisition comes at cost of margin compression, potential inability to invest in product, and risk of training market to compete on price rather than value.

buddhi_options.option: Acquire or merge with the competitor to eliminate pricing pressure and consolidate market position.
buddhi_options.reasoning: Eliminates immediate threat but may be financially overextended; raises integration risks and cultural concerns about rapid growth at potential expense of product quality.

decision_rationale: The selected option acknowledges real customer retention risk while positioning the company for long-term competitive advantage through differentiation rather than unsustainable price competition. By selectively matching pricing on entry products while accelerating feature development, the company preserved margins, retained key customers, and ultimately launched capabilities that became market-leading within 18 months.

scenario: A mid-sized manufacturing company discovers that a key supplier uses child labor in subcontracted mills. Replacing the supplier costs 23% more and delays production by 6 months, affecting customer contracts and Q4 earnings. Legal counsel says current contracts have no explicit anti-labor clauses.

manas_signals.description: Fear of reputational damage if labor practices go public; fear of customer backlash and activist campaigns.
Pattern of competitors using similar suppliers without immediate consequences; normalized low-cost practices across industry.
Risk of supply chain disruption, customer contract penalties, and erosion of market share during transition period.
Desire to maintain shareholder returns and executive bonuses tied to profit margins.

buddhi_options.option: Immediately audit and terminate supplier contract; absorb 6-month delay and 23% cost increase; communicate transparently with customers.
buddhi_options.reasoning: Honors human dignity and legal/moral duty. Short-term pain but restores integrity and builds long-term customer loyalty among values-aligned buyers.

buddhi_options.option: Privately pressure supplier to improve labor practices over 18 months; audit quarterly; maintain contract contingent on compliance.
buddhi_options.reasoning: Attempts remediation while managing transition costs. Risk of greenwashing if practices don't genuinely improve; lacks public accountability.

buddhi_options.option: Continue with current supplier; implement internal compliance documentation; invest in carbon offsetting initiatives instead.
buddhi_options.reasoning: Avoids financial pain but moral hazard is severe. Treats unrelated ESG spending as substitute for addressing direct harm.

buddhi_options.option: Negotiate phased transition: 9-month dual sourcing with 12% cost premium; supplier commits to third-party labor audits.
buddhi_options.reasoning: Balances speed of ethical remediation with operational feasibility. Third-party verification adds credibility. Requires robust accountability mechanisms.

decision_rationale: The immediate termination option aligns with both moral principle and enlightened self-interest: customer trust and regulatory trends increasingly penalize labor exploitation. Short-term shareholder pressure is temporary; reputational recovery from exposure would be far costlier.

    Reasoning: Avoids financial pain but moral hazard is severe. Treats unrelated ESG spending as substitute for addressing direct harm.
  Option 3 (dharma=0.7): Negotiate phased transition: 9-month dual sourcing with 12% cost premium; supplier commits to third-party labor audits.
    Reasoning: Balances speed of ethical remediation with operational feasibility. Third-party verification adds credibility. Requires robust accountability mechanisms.
[DECISION] The immediate termination option aligns with both moral principle and enlightened self-interest: customer trust and regulatory trends increasingly penalize labor exploitation. Short-term shareholder pressure is temporary; reputational recovery from exposure would be far costlier.
[OUTCOME] 0.71

scenario: A nonprofit education organization must reallocate $200K from its annual budget as a grant funding stream dried up unexpectedly. Current spend: $80K classroom programs, $60K teacher training, $40K administrative overhead, $20K community outreach. Leadership debates whether to cut across all areas equally or make strategic cuts to protect core mission.

manas_signals.description: Fear that cutting classroom programs will directly harm the students and communities the organization serves.
Desire to maintain teacher training quality to preserve long-term program effectiveness and staff capability.
Pattern of protecting administrative overhead in past budget cycles despite mission-first rhetoric.

buddhi_options.option: Cut classroom programs 20%, teacher training 10%, outreach 30%, eliminate $8K admin overhead—preserve direct teaching and trainer capacity.
buddhi_options.reasoning: Aligns cuts with mission priority (impact on students) and operational reality (trained teachers enable programs); overhead reduction demonstrates commitment to efficiency.

buddhi_options.option: Cut all areas proportionally by 25%—fair and simple, avoids conflict and difficult choices.
buddhi_options.reasoning: Appears neutral but actually abandons mission prioritization; equal cuts harm core programs and undermine teacher development, the foundation of long-term impact.

buddhi_options.option: Pause classroom expansion, maintain all current programs, reduce overhead by $20K and fundraise aggressively—avoid difficult cuts.
buddhi_options.reasoning: Delays problem-solving and relies on uncertain revenue; realistic but doesn't address immediate $200K shortfall.

decision_rationale: Option 0 reflects clear dharma by anchoring cuts to mission impact rather than organizational comfort. Low complexity allows confident elevation to target 1, where sustained integrity matters more than short-term morale. High outcome score reflects that this decision, though painful, preserves the organization's core capacity and signals genuine mission commitment to stakeholders.

scenario: A mid-level manager discovers that a senior executive is inflating project metrics to secure continued funding for their department. The manager has evidence but reporting it could damage relationships and risk their own advancement. However, staying silent means the organization will continue investing in underperforming initiatives based on false data.

manas_signals.description: Fear of retaliation and career consequences if the executive is exposed and feels threatened.
Desire to protect organizational integrity and prevent waste of shareholder resources.
Risk of being labeled a whistleblower and facing workplace isolation or termination.
Pattern of overlooking minor infractions in exchange for loyalty has normalized ethical shortcuts in the culture.

buddhi_options.option: Report confidentially through the ethics hotline or board audit committee with documented evidence, protecting identity and following formal channels.
buddhi_options.reasoning: Upholds organizational integrity and truth while using established safeguards designed for whistleblowers; balances duty to company with personal protection.

buddhi_options.option: Privately confront the executive and request they self-correct within 30 days before any external reporting.
buddhi_options.reasoning: Honors relationship and gives opportunity for redemption, but risks being dismissed or manipulated; places burden of correction on perpetrator.

buddhi_options.option: Do nothing, as reporting feels too risky personally and the executive will eventually be caught by auditors anyway.
buddhi_options.reasoning: Avoids immediate personal risk but abdicates responsibility; assumes external audits will catch fraud, which is not guaranteed.

buddhi_options.option: Share concerns with a trusted peer in another department to validate before deciding on action.
buddhi_options.reasoning: Seeks consensus and validation but risks gossip, broader exposure, and dilutes accountability chain; delays necessary action.

decision_rationale: Choosing the ethics hotline respects both truth and personal safety by leveraging institutional mechanisms designed for this scenario. This decision elevates the manager's character by prioritizing systemic integrity over comfort, while the formal process protects against the fear-driven paralysis that often enables organizational dysfunction.

scenario: A mid-sized SaaS company must allocate Q3 budget between expanding QA automation (high upfront cost, long ROI) versus hiring two senior engineers for feature velocity. The product team argues features drive revenue; QA warns that technical debt is causing 15% of production incidents. Leadership must decide within 48 hours.

manas_signals.description: QA leadership fears mounting production incidents will damage customer trust and churn if automation isn't prioritized.
Product leadership desires rapid feature delivery to meet board revenue targets and competitive pressure.
Historical pattern shows features generate short-term wins but technical debt compounds exponentially over 18 months.
Risk of customer churn from incidents vs. risk of market share loss from slower feature delivery.

buddhi_options.option: Allocate 70% to senior engineers, 30% to QA automation tooling; establish SLA for incident reduction within 6 months or rebalance.
buddhi_options.reasoning: Honors both immediate business need and quality foundation; creates accountability mechanism to prevent debt snowballing.

buddhi_options.option: Fully fund QA automation now; defer two senior hires to Q4 pending automation ROI metrics.
buddhi_options.reasoning: Addresses root cause (incident prevention) first; automation benefits scale; deferred hiring preserves flexibility and prevents compounding risk.

buddhi_options.option: Hire one senior engineer; outsource QA automation to vendor to split costs and reduce internal overhead.
buddhi_options.reasoning: Appears balanced but fragments ownership, reduces learning, and vendor lock-in creates hidden costs; half-measures solve neither problem deeply.

buddhi_options.option: Hire both senior engineers; implement lightweight automation framework in-house using existing junior engineers' capacity.
buddhi_options.reasoning: Overloads junior engineers with unfamiliar work, likely delays both automation and feature work; ignores expertise gap.

decision_rationale: Option 2 prioritizes long-term system health by solving the incident root cause first, enabling senior engineers to build on a stable foundation in Q4. This honors both dharma (quality) and artha (business), while preventing false economy of short-term hiring that masks deeper problems.

scenario: A specialty chemicals manufacturer is facing margin pressure from commodity cost inflation and pricing power limitations. The CFO proposes automating 60% of the manufacturing workforce through robotics and advanced process control systems, a $40M capital investment with 7-year payback period. The CEO is concerned about the social impact on the company's loyal 300-person workforce, family-owned brand reputation built on craftsmanship, and stranded assets if market demand declines. The board is pushing for return-to-profitability through cost reduction.

manas_signals.description: Leadership fears both the social impact of job displacement and the risk of a massive capital investment in a commodity market with uncertain demand.
Automation commitment creates fixed costs and inflexibility; if demand declines or new competitors emerge, company becomes locked into high-fixed-cost structure.
Board and CFO are driven to improve margins and ROI to satisfy investors; attractive returns justify the investment on financial criteria.
Specialty chemical manufacturers that have successfully automated have achieved 5-7% margin improvement; those that failed to automate have experienced gradual market share loss.

buddhi_options.option: Implement full automation strategy with phased approach, investing $40M over 5 years while offering comprehensive retraining and career transition support to displaced workers.
buddhi_options.reasoning: Addresses business necessity for cost competitiveness while honoring workforce commitment through thoughtful transition support; high capital commitment but aligns with long-term market trends.

buddhi_options.option: Reject automation strategy and instead invest in premium market repositioning, moving upmarket to higher-margin specialty grades where automation is less critical and brand value matters more.
buddhi_options.reasoning: Preserves workforce and brand values while seeking sustainable margin improvement; requires market and product innovation capability but may be more aligned with company identity.

buddhi_options.option: Implement selective automation in high-volume, low-complexity product lines while maintaining skilled workforce for custom and small-batch specialty products that command premium pricing.
buddhi_options.reasoning: Balances cost efficiency with workforce preservation and brand positioning; creates portfolio strategy that leverages different competitive advantages and reduces workforce displacement.

buddhi_options.option: Implement full automation aggressively to achieve maximum ROI, minimize severance costs, and redeploy capital to growth opportunities, accepting social externalities as business necessity.
buddhi_options.reasoning: Prioritizes financial returns over workforce welfare; violates implicit stakeholder commitments and contradicts brand positioning as values-driven family company.

decision_rationale: The selective automation approach was chosen because it aligned cost competitiveness with brand values and preserved organizational culture. The company automated commodity high-volume product lines while investing in premium custom products that justified higher labor costs and created new roles in application engineering and customer support. This strategy required significant product and marketing investment but ultimately improved margins by 6%, while reducing workforce by 18% instead of 60%. The approach reinforced brand positioning as values-driven and resulted in stronger employee loyalty during the transition, though it delivered slower margin improvement than full automation would have.

scenario: Your head of engineering proposes hiring a brilliant but controversial figure: a former executive with exceptional technical leadership but documented pattern of creating hostile work environments for junior women engineers. Your company has a strong diversity commitment and recent culture survey showed trust issues around leadership accountability. Hiring them could accelerate product roadmap; rejecting them signals limited tolerance for complex hires.

manas_signals.description: Technical capability gap creates pressure to acquire top talent quickly to hit engineering milestones.
Hiring this person could trigger attrition among junior engineers and undermine stated values around psychological safety.
Documented behavior pattern indicates systemic issue, not isolated incident; past does not suggest change without intervention.
Recruiter emphasizes candidate's technical merits while downplaying cultural concerns as 'learning opportunity.'

buddhi_options.option: Reject the hire; recruit two complementary engineers with aligned values; accept 2-3 month timeline extension.
buddhi_options.reasoning: Protects culture integrity and junior talent; sends clear signal about values; longer timeline but sustainable.

buddhi_options.option: Hire with explicit behavioral expectations, executive coaching, and 90-day review with clear exit criteria tied to culture feedback.
buddhi_options.reasoning: Allows opportunity for growth and change; maintains benefit of technical skill; requires disciplined accountability and honest review process.

buddhi_options.option: Make offer contingent on working with external culture consultant and monthly 360 feedback; set high bar for continued employment.
buddhi_options.reasoning: Conditional approach shows willingness to take calculated risk; structures accountability; still requires team's psychological burden.

buddhi_options.option: Hire for advisory/consulting role only; test cultural fit without full integration; convert to full-time if successful.
buddhi_options.reasoning: Minimizes risk but may appear evasive; delays deeper integration; doesn't fully resolve the core dilemma.

decision_rationale: Technical capability is important but not at the cost of psychological safety for junior engineers. Pattern of behavior, not isolated incident, suggests low probability of genuine change without external intervention. Recruiting takes longer but preserves the culture foundation that attracts other strong talent.
